topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into an incomprehensible mess. *Application unsuccessful.* The official stamp of rejection felt cold and sharp against my chest. My hands, which had been gripping the mouse so tightly I could feel the strain in my knuckles, slowly relaxed, then trembled as I reread the rejection letter for the third time. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat, a sound halfway between a sigh and a choked sob. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding deafening in the quiet dorm room. I walked to the window, pulling the thin curtain aside, and watched the bustling campus life below. Everyone seemed so vibrant, so certain of their paths, while I felt utterly adrift, the carefully constructed scaffolding of my future suddenly crumbling into dust.

[story 2]

"No," I whispered to the empty room, the sound thin and reedy. I crumpled the rejection packet in my fist, the glossy paper protesting under the pressure. My jaw clenched so hard I could taste copper. Every muscle in my body seemed to have seized up; my stomach twisted into a tight, nauseous knot. I paced the small rug, the rhythm erratic and frantic. I stopped abruptly by my desk, leaning my forehead against the cool wood. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I wanted to shout, to argue with the invisible committee, but the words caught in my throat, lodging there as a painful lump. I slid down onto the edge of my bed, burying my face in my arms, letting the heavy weight of disappointment settle deep into my bones.

[story 3]

I reread the decision, focusing intently on the precise phrasing of the reasons given. Each sentence was a tiny shard of glass, digging into my composure. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid, as I tried to draw in enough air to steady myself. I stood in the middle of the common area, my shoulders hunched forward as if bracing against an unseen impact. I kept touching the smooth surface of my phone, scrolling through old acceptance letters, each one a vivid reminder of what might have been. A tremor ran through my hands, making the screen flicker momentarily. When I finally dropped the phone onto the sofa cushion, the resulting soft thud seemed to echo the sudden hollowness in my core. I just stood there, rigid, waiting for something to shift, anything to change the sharp, unpleasant pressure building behind my eyes.
